text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Procurement and Vendor Oversight lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: AI procurement requires public health departments to evaluate vendor claims, contract terms, data rights, model documentation, auditability, accessibility, cybersecurity, support, and exit plans before an AI-enabled product is approved.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.

AI procurement requires public health departments to evaluate vendor claims, contract terms, data rights, model documentation, auditability, accessibility, cybersecurity, support, and exit plans before an AI-enabled product is approved.
textPreview: AI procurement requires public health departments to evaluate vendor claims, contract terms, data rights, model documentation, auditabili...

text: Most departments will buy or inherit AI-enabled systems before they build their own. Vendor oversight protects public health authority, data rights, security, equity, and operational continuity.
textPreview: Most departments will buy or inherit AI-enabled systems before they build their own. Vendor oversight protects public health authority, d...

text: Vendor AI should not bypass governance, privacy, security, accessibility, validation, or public accountability requirements.
textPreview: Vendor AI should not bypass governance, privacy, security, accessibility, validation, or public accountability requirements.

text:
Use this slide in the Procurement and Vendor Oversight training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 7, 8, 11, 12 and Tools 11, 13, 15, 34, 40, 43.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Vendor AI should not bypass governance, privacy, security, accessibility, validation, or public accountability requirements.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Procurement and Vendor Oversight in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Procurement and Vendor Oversight training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 7, 8, 11, 12 and Tools 11, 13, 15, 34, 40, 43.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Public health departments often encounter AI through vendor platforms, not custom-built internal systems. Procurement staff, program owners, IT, security, privacy, legal, and governance members need shared criteria for evaluating vendor claims before contracts are signed or pilots begin.

Clear description of what AI capability is included and whether it is generative, predictive, agentic, or rules-based. Model documentation, validation evidence, limitations, monitoring approach, and known failure modes. Contract terms covering.

text: What data will the vendor receive, store, process, or use to improve models? Can the department audit performance, errors, bias, access logs, and system changes? What happens if the model changes, performance declines, guidance changes, or the.

text: Route vendor AI proposals through governance before pilot approval. Use risk tiering to decide what validation, privacy, legal, and procurement review is required. Do not accept vendor performance claims without evidence, test data, monitoring.

For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

What AI-enabled vendor tools are already in use or under consideration? Which contract terms would prevent the department from auditing or safely exiting the system? Who must review vendor claims before a pilot or purchase is approved?

Expected takeaways include: Vendor AI review checklist, Procurement questions for governance, Draft AI contract requirements.
